reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Strong three-tier conceptual breakdown (viewing/controlling/reducing) with a table that tells the reader exactly which page to visit — this is the most valuable structural choice on the page.
Opening paragraph is tightly scoped and self-contained (≈57 words), clearly states the AWS-bills-you / Stacktape-is-separate distinction up front before the reader has to ask.
The 'Stacktape subscription vs. AWS costs' comparison table is concrete and removes a common first-time confusion about who bills what.
Budget alert section accurately reflects the two scopes (org-wide vs. per-stack) and their different activation requirements, consistent with Explanations.tsx source.
FAQ has exactly 10 questions spanning all three required categories — Stacktape-specific, AWS-service use-case, and choosing/comparison. Coverage is balanced.
Console dashboard description (KPI card, by-project chart with top-10 plus Other bucket, by-resource-type chart, sparkline) is faithfully grounded in OverviewPage.tsx source.
Internal linking is consistent and complete — every mention of a sub-page links to it.
The opinionated <Tip> — 'Start with a single org-wide budget … add per-stack budgets later' — gives a first-time user a concrete first step rather than leaving them to choose.
problems: The 'A fourth page, Per-resource breakdown, bridges viewing and reducing' sentence appears after the three-row table, which has already defined three concerns as exhaustive. A first-time reader will be confused about whether the table is the full picture. The fourth item should either be a row in the table or removed from prose that follows a table framed as the complete taxonomy.

The 'Viewing costs' section mentions 'a connected AWS account' without explaining what account connection means to a first-time user who hasn't set one up. This is jargon-as-prerequisite for a reader who may not know they need to take an account-connection step before cost data appears at all.

The 'Reducing costs' section gives five bullet points but no triage signal — a reader with a Lambda-heavy architecture gets no more guidance than one with a database-heavy architecture. Without a one-sentence steer ('most teams start with compute right-sizing'), the list reads as an undifferentiated menu.

The 'costs-project' gadget in OverviewPage.tsx is titled 'AWS Costs by Project' in the GADGETS array but internally calls trpc.overviewCostsByService — the doc faithfully follows the UI label, but a reader who navigates to the Console will see a chart labeled 'AWS Costs by Project' that may actually show AWS service breakdown. This source ambiguity is not the doc's fault, but the prose description ('cost-by-project chart') will collide with what the reader sees if the chart shows service names rather than project names.
optionalImprovements: Fold the 'Per-resource breakdown' fourth page into the three-concern table as a fourth row, or reframe the table intro as 'three primary concerns — a fourth page covers attribution detail' so the table does not imply it is exhaustive.

Add one sentence to the 'Viewing costs' section explaining what 'connected AWS account' means in practical terms (e.g., 'Your AWS account must be connected to Stacktape via a cross-account IAM role — see [Connected AWS accounts](/stacktape-console/aws-accounts) if you haven't done this yet').

In the 'Reducing costs' section, add a one-sentence triage lead before the bullet list — e.g., 'For most teams, compute right-sizing and deleting unused stages provide the fastest payback; databases and CDN caching matter once you have sustained traffic.' This turns a menu into a guide.

The H2 'Three concerns: viewing, controlling, reducing' uses an unusual colon-plus-list format. A shorter label ('Cost management pillars' or simply 'Overview') would scan better and stay within the 1–5 word guideline, with the table beneath carrying the full enumeration.

FAQ answer for 'How do I reduce Lambda costs?' cites '~20% cheaper per GB-second' for Graviton — this is a well-known AWS figure and the FAQ rules permit it, but adding a hedge ('roughly 20%, per AWS pricing') signals the number may shift and is consistent with the pricing wording guidelines.

The closing FAQ answer ('Can I export cost data?') correctly redirects to AWS Cost Explorer, but a first-time reader may not know how to find their Cost and Usage Reports in S3. Adding 'AWS Cost Explorer is accessible from the AWS Console under Billing → Cost Explorer' would make the answer actionable without straying from sourced facts.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: The three-concern framework (viewing / controlling / reducing) maps directly to what the reader needs and the table presentation makes it immediately scannable.

Cost data delay and the daily AWS Cost and Usage Report update cycle are stated precisely and matched against both the Explanations.tsx tooltip and the CostsAndUsagePage source — no invented behavior.
Budget scopes (organization-wide vs. per-stack) and the redeploy-required caveat for stack-scoped budgets are accurately pulled from the Explanations.tsx budgets tooltip.
The Stacktape subscription vs. AWS costs table is a strong, concrete disambiguation that directly answers a common reader question.
FAQ covers all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, underlying AWS service, choosing/comparison) with appropriate answer depth.
No forbidden patterns: no YAML config blocks, no StacktapeConfig type, no fenced TypeScript with defineConfig, no PropertiesTable, no duplicate ApiReference.
Overview dashboard details (KPI card with trend label, top-10 costs-by-project chart with Other bucket, costs-by-resource chart, sparklines) are grounded in OverviewPage.tsx source code.
CostsAndUsagePage source correctly described: AWS account selector, month selector, sortable table with project/stage/region/cost columns, row-click navigation.
problems: Teams and Discord are listed as alert channel options in the 'Controlling costs with budgets' section and FAQ, but the Explanations.tsx source only names 'a Slack channel, an email address, or a webhook'. Adding Teams and Discord is unsupported by the provided source and would mislead a reader who configures channels and can't find those options.

The overview dashboard section calls the gadget 'cost-by-project chart', which matches the gadget title ('AWS Costs by Project') but the underlying data comes from the tRPC endpoint overviewCostsByService whose response model uses a 'services' array. The draft doesn't disambiguate whether 'project' here means Stacktape projects or AWS service types — the naming is internally inconsistent in the source and the page should hedge rather than assert 'top 10 projects'.
mandatoryFixes: Remove 'Teams' and 'Discord' from the alert channel list in the 'Controlling costs with budgets' section and the FAQ answer about budget alerts. The Explanations.tsx source enumerates only Slack, email, and webhook as channel destinations. Replace the claim with 'Slack, email, webhook, and other configured alert channels' or link to the alert channels reference page; do not name providers that are not grounded in the provided source.
optionalImprovements: Clarify 'cost-by-project chart' by noting that the chart groups costs by the top 10 line items in the AWS cost report for the organization (as reflected by the 'services' data model in the source), rather than asserting they are Stacktape projects — this avoids confusing readers who see AWS service names in the chart.

The 'Reducing costs' section lists five levers in prose but never links to the optimization-tips sub-page until the end of the section. Consider moving the link to the top of the section so readers who want action can jump directly.

The Info callout under 'Viewing costs' restates the 24-hour delay already mentioned in the 'How AWS cost data works' section. Consolidate or trim the repeat to avoid redundancy without losing the signal.

The FAQ answer 'Can I export cost data?' tells readers to access raw S3 reports or use AWS Cost Explorer — useful, but it could also mention that the Stacktape Console costs page covers the common use case and when to go outside the Console (custom date ranges, cross-account roll-ups, raw data pipelines), which gives more decision-making context.

The frontmatter seoDescription is accurate and specific — no change needed — but the seoTitle uses a pipe separator ('|') while many Stacktape pages use em-dashes or colons. Verify consistency with other pages in the managing-costs section.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Self-contained, concrete opening paragraph that directly states AWS bills the user's account, Stacktape does not mark up pricing, and names the four platform capabilities — an extractable snippet AI engines can quote verbatim.
The 'three concerns' table is well-structured and immediately actionable: it maps each concern to a plain question and a destination page, which is the right orientation pattern for an overview page.

Source-grounded UI specifics: the 'top 10 projects plus an Other bucket' chart detail, the sortable cost table with Project / Stage / Region / Month-to-date columns, and the AWS account and month selectors are all directly confirmed by CostsByProjectChart and CostsAndUsagePage.tsx.
The Stacktape subscription vs. AWS costs table near the bottom is a clean, extractable comparison that answers a question readers frequently ask — what am I actually paying for and to whom.

FAQ covers all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS-service use-case, choosing/comparison) with 10 well-formed questions, and most answers are 2-4 sentence paragraphs suitable as featured snippets.
The daily-update caveat is surfaced twice (Info callout + FAQ) at appropriate points — readers who skim the UI section and readers who jump to the FAQ both see it.

Opening paragraph explicitly names Stacktape Console as the interface, names the four capabilities (dashboards, per-stack attribution, budget alerts, optimization guidance), and flags the separate subscription fee — all in under 75 words.
problems: The claim 'Stacktape tags every resource it creates, and those tags map costs back to specific projects, stages, and resource types' is not confirmed in the provided source files. The Explanations.tsx uses the phrase 'deployment metadata', not 'tags'. The attribution mechanism is inferred, not stated.

Alert channel types enumerated — 'Slack, email, Teams, Discord, or a webhook' — appear twice (body and FAQ) but none of the provided source files enumerate these channel types. The Explanations.tsx mentions 'channels' as a concept without naming types. This is an unsupported specific enumeration.

The '/managing-costs/per-resource-breakdown' link is presented as a real peer page but no such page is confirmed in the source. The per-resource cost chart (CostsByResourceChart) lives inside the Overview dashboard, not a separate page. Inventing a page link misleads readers and breaks navigation.

The '/managing-costs/dashboards', '/managing-costs/optimization-tips' child page links are also not confirmed in the source. Linking to unconfirmed pages as though they exist is a concrete correctness problem, not just a style note.

Some H2 headings are slightly verbose: 'How AWS cost data works' (5+ words), 'Controlling costs with budgets' (4 words plus qualifier), 'Cost attribution by resource type' (5 words). Short noun-form labels ('Cost data', 'Budget alerts', 'Attribution') would scan better.

The 'Reducing costs' section is a bare numbered list with no per-item depth or cost/tradeoff signal. As an overview section this is acceptable, but the list has no prose anchoring any item to a concrete scenario or magnitude, which hurts extractability for individual items like 'CDN caching' or 'Choosing the right database tier'.
mandatoryFixes: Replace 'Stacktape tags every resource it creates, and those tags map costs back to specific projects, stages, and resource types' with the source-grounded phrasing from Explanations.tsx: attribution relies on 'deployment metadata' — Stacktape tracks which stacks were deployed via Stacktape and links costs to those stacks. Do not claim tagging as the mechanism unless a source file confirms it.

Remove or hedge the enumeration of alert channel types ('Slack, email, Teams, Discord, or a webhook') in the body and FAQ. These channel types do not appear in any provided source file. Replace with 'your configured alert channels' and link to the alert channels page, which is the pattern used in Explanations.tsx.

Remove or replace the '/managing-costs/per-resource-breakdown' link. The per-resource cost breakdown (CostsByResourceChart) is part of the Overview dashboard page, not a separate page. The draft incorrectly presents it as a fourth peer page. Rewrite the 'Three concerns' section to describe it as a chart within the overview dashboard, not a standalone page.

Verify that '/managing-costs/dashboards' and '/managing-costs/optimization-tips' are real routes before linking to them. If they are not confirmed, replace with prose descriptions or remove the links. Do not invent navigation destinations on a page that will be served to real readers.
optionalImprovements: Shorten verbose H2 headings: 'How AWS cost data works' → 'Cost data'; 'Controlling costs with budgets' → 'Budget alerts'; 'Cost attribution by resource type' → 'Attribution'. Shorter labels improve scannability and TOC rendering.

Add one sentence of context per item in the 'Reducing costs' numbered list — e.g., for 'Scaling to zero': 'Lambda functions and container services with scale-to-zero configured incur no compute cost during idle periods.' This makes individual list items extractable as cited snippets rather than context-dependent bullets.

The FAQ answer to 'How can I reduce Lambda costs?' mentions ARM/Graviton being ~20% cheaper per GB-second. If this number is not in the provided source, soften it to 'typically lower cost per GB-second' to stay within source-grounded bounds for a Stacktape-specific claim.

Consider adding a brief note in the 'Viewing costs' section that the KPI card on the overview dashboard also shows a trend sparkline across recent months (confirmed by costTrendSparkline in OverviewPage.tsx) — this is a concrete, extractable detail currently missing from the prose description of the overview dashboard.

The budget body section describes 'Created immediately when you save the budget configuration' for org-wide budgets. This is grounded in Explanations.tsx ('Organization-wide budget alerts are created in the selected AWS account when you save them') — good. Consider mirroring the exact source phrasing more closely for source-grounding verifier confidence.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is largely well-grounded against the three provided source files (OverviewPage.tsx, CostsAndUsagePage.tsx, Explanations.tsx). Cost data latency, budget scopes, overview dashboard components, and the costs-per-stack table are all accurately described. Cross-links are valid against the navigation index. The main issues are: the opening paragraph attributes "optimization guidance" to the Console (unsupported by Console source), a specific mechanism claim about resource tagging that the source describes more generically as "deployment metadata", and a misleading FAQ assertion that Stacktape has "no access to your billing" when the Console clearly reads cost data from the AWS account.

issues.statement: The Stacktape Console gives you dashboards, per-stack cost attribution, budget alerts, and optimization guidance so you can understand, control, and reduce your AWS spend without leaving the platform.
issues.reason: The source files show the Console provides dashboards (OverviewPage.tsx), per-stack cost attribution (CostsAndUsagePage.tsx), and budget alerts (BudgetStatusPanel in OverviewPage.tsx). However, no Console source file shows an 'optimization guidance' feature. The /managing-costs/optimization-tips page exists in the docs navigation, but it is a documentation page — not a Console feature. Attributing it to 'The Stacktape Console gives you...' is unsupported.
issues.suggestedFix: Change to: 'The Stacktape Console gives you dashboards, per-stack cost attribution, and budget alerts so you can understand and control your AWS spend without leaving the platform. This section also covers optimization guidance to help you reduce costs.'

issues.statement: The data is attributed to your stacks using deployment metadata — Stacktape tags every resource it creates, and those tags map costs back to specific projects, stages, and resource types.
issues.reason: The Explanations.tsx source says 'Stack-scoped budget status depends on Stacktape deployment metadata' and CostsAndUsagePage.tsx references 'costAndUsageBreakdown?.attributed?.stacks'. The source uses the term 'deployment metadata' but never specifies 'tags' as the mechanism. While AWS resource tagging is the standard cost-attribution mechanism, the claim that 'Stacktape tags every resource it creates' is not stated in these source files and introduces a specific mechanism assertion that may not hold for all resource types.
issues.suggestedFix: Change to: 'The data is attributed to your stacks using deployment metadata that Stacktape attaches during deployment, mapping costs back to specific projects, stages, and resource types.' This keeps the factual claim without asserting the specific tagging mechanism.

evidence.quote: Stack-scoped budget status depends on Stacktape deployment metadata, so deploy matching stacks after creating or changing stack scoped alerts.

issues.statement: Stacktape has no access to your billing and does not mark up or intermediate AWS charges.
issues.reason: The Console clearly reads cost data from the connected AWS account — OverviewPage.tsx queries overviewCostsByService, overviewCostsByResourceType, overviewCostTrend, and overviewBudgetStatus; CostsAndUsagePage.tsx queries awsAccountCostReports. Saying Stacktape has 'no access to your billing' is misleading when it has read access to cost data. The intended meaning is likely that Stacktape does not handle AWS payments, but the phrasing overstates the separation.
issues.suggestedFix: Change to: 'Stacktape does not handle or intermediate your AWS charges — AWS bills you directly at standard pricing. Stacktape reads cost data from your connected AWS account for display in the Console.'

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: First paragraph is self-contained, fact-dense, and correctly explains the billing separation between AWS charges and Stacktape subscription — ideal for AI overview extraction.
Intro navigation table (Viewing / Controlling / Reducing) is highly citation-friendly and gives AI engines a clean structured answer to 'what does this section cover'.

H2 'Stacktape charges vs. AWS charges' is 6 words and somewhat sentence-like. Noun-form labels of 1-5 words ('Billing', 'Charges') scan better and comply with the heading discipline rules.

The 'Reducing costs' section uses a numbered prose list of five levers with no structured comparison. A table (lever | what it does | when to apply) would be ~2-4x more likely to be cited by AI engines and would satisfy the per-variant equal-depth expectation if each lever were treated consistently.

The overview dashboard's costs-by-project gadget is backed by a `overviewCostsByService` tRPC query (service-level AWS data) while the GADGETS title is 'AWS Costs by Project'. The page describes it as 'costs-by-project chart' without acknowledging that the underlying data may group by AWS service name rather than Stacktape project name — a potential source ambiguity that readers encountering the chart may notice.

This keeps the paragraph fully self-contained for AI extraction.

Shorten H2 headings to 1-4 word noun labels: 'Stacktape charges vs. AWS charges' → 'Billing', 'Controlling costs with budgets' → 'Budget alerts'. The peer headings 'Viewing costs' and 'Reducing costs' set a two-word pattern; following it improves TOC consistency.

Convert the 'Reducing costs' numbered list into a markdown table with columns: Lever | What it does | When to apply. This makes the section more scannable, more citation-friendly, and gives each lever consistent treatment depth.

Add a one-sentence clarification in the 'Viewing costs' section noting that the costs-by-project gadget groups data by AWS service name as reported in Cost and Usage Reports — readers who expect Stacktape project names may be confused if the chart shows AWS service categories.

The redundant closing sentence in the 'Cost attribution' H3 ('When you deploy a stack through Stacktape, the metadata is attached automatically — no additional configuration is needed.') could be trimmed or merged into the preceding sentence to avoid padding.

Consider adding a one-line pricing context near the top (as the style guide recommends for resource pages) — e.g., noting that AWS Cost and Usage Reports themselves are free but that the underlying AWS services have their own charges billed directly. This sets expectation for readers who are new to AWS cost monitoring.

The FAQ answer for 'How can I reduce Lambda function costs?' cites Graviton being 'approximately 20% cheaper per GB-second than x86'. This is a well-known AWS fact and is acceptable per the FAQ grounding rules, but adding the caveat 'according to AWS published pricing' would insulate the claim against future price changes.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

Budget section covers both organization-wide and per-stack variants with honest tradeoffs (org-wide activates immediately; per-stack requires a redeploy) and a clear opinionated recommendation for new users.
Reducing costs section gives concrete, realistic comparisons — 512 MB vs 1,024 MB Lambda, `db.t4g.micro` vs Aurora Serverless — making the guidance actionable rather than generic.

The style guide requires a meaningful spread across all three categories.

The Cost attribution subsection claims 'When you deploy a stack through Stacktape, the metadata is attached automatically — no additional configuration is needed.' The provided source (Explanations.tsx) says this only for budget scope ('Stack-scoped budget status depends on Stacktape deployment metadata, so deploy matching stacks after creating or changing stack scoped alerts') — not for cost attribution in general. A sentence acknowledging that the overview is a mixed dashboard — and that cost KPIs always reflect the current month while deployment charts use a selectable time range (7d / 14d / 30d / 90d / 6m / 1y) — would prevent reader confusion when they see different date scopes on the same page.
optionalImprovements: Rebalance the FAQ: replace 2–3 Stacktape-specific questions with AWS-service questions readers actually search for — e.g., 'How does AWS Cost and Usage Report work?', 'What is the difference between AWS Budgets and Cost Explorer?', 'How does AWS tag-based cost allocation work?' — to better cover category 2 (underlying AWS service mechanics).

Soften or qualify the cost attribution claim: instead of 'no additional configuration is needed', write something like 'Stacktape attaches deployment metadata when a stack is deployed — this is what powers per-stack attribution' and link to the budgets page for the authoritative statement about when to redeploy after adding stack-scoped budget alerts.

Add a one-sentence note that the overview dashboard shows two date scopes simultaneously: cost data is always current-month, while the deployment stats section uses a selectable time range. This prevents confusion when readers see different date windows on the same dashboard (grounded in OverviewPage.tsx's `TIME_RANGE_TABS` for stats vs `new Date()` anchor for costs).

The <Info> callout 'New stacks may take up to a day to appear in cost data after their first deployment' duplicates the earlier prose in the same section ('Reports are generated daily...may take up to a day to appear'). Either integrate it into the prose or remove the callout to avoid redundancy.

Consider noting that the costs-per-stack table is scoped to one connected AWS account at a time (account selector is required before data loads), which matters for organizations with multiple connected AWS accounts — grounded in CostsAndUsagePage.tsx's account selector logic.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
